title_content.content: 1.1 The need for an Orlicz topology

paragraph_content.content: be a separable Hilbert space of infinite dimension, and
of all normal states can be furnished with the trace norm. However, the associated metric is not a good measure of the distance between states. For example, if
contains a dense set of states of infinite entropy. These states cannot be near
in any physical sense. Moreover, if
is the dynamics of a system, then we expect that
is the relative entropy of Umegaki. This, which is related to the free energy, should be finite for physical states. We need a stronger topology, such that a 'hood of
. In this paper we show that this goal can be achieved by a norm topology, by developing an analogue of the
space: the unwanted states near a given state

are outside the space of finite norm. The norm is a limiting case of the Schatten cross norms on spaces of compact operators, which can be regarded as quantum version of Orlicz spaces. Orlicz spaces were first introduced into information geometry in the classical case by Pistone and Sempi [15].

title_content.content: 1.2 The work of Pistone and Sempi

paragraph_content.content: These authors are statisticians, who developed a theory of best estimators (of minimum variance) among all locally unbiased estimators in non-parametric estimation for classical statistical theory.
the density of a probability measure equivalent to

The Cramér class of random variables was shown by Pistone and Sempi to be a Banach space, and so, to be complete, when furnished with the Luxemburg norm

to consist of all distributions in some exponential family,
runs over the Cramér class. They add similar 'hoods for each

Legendre transforms come into their own in the context of a manifold, as a transform between the tangent space and the cotangent space at each point. There is only one manifold, but many coordinatizations. For the information manifold
of Pistone and Sempi, the points of the manifold are the probability measures
, and these form a positive cone inside
. This cone can be coordinatized by the Radon-Nikodym derivatives

title_content.content: 2.3 The category of partially ordered Riemannian manifolds

paragraph_content.content: Amari has posed the question [2], what properties, extra to being a Riemannian manifold, characterise information manifolds? Obviously, such a manifold must possess the Amari family of affine connections,

relative to the metric. One could ask the same question for quantum information manifolds. These affine connections are associated with the embeddings (17), which can be extended to weights (non-normalised probabilities) and have quantum versions
The quantum versions of the limit cases,
It is a fact that all these maps are operator monotone; they preserve the partial order between operators. We say
is a positive (semidefinite) operator. Let us say that a coordinate system

for the set of weights is a monotone coordinate system if this partial ordering is preserved. An allowed coordinate system for the quantum case must be monotone, and a morphism between two information manifolds must involve monotone maps. This differs from Chentsov's definition of morphism; it allows non-linear changes of coordinates, which transform one monotone metric to another [10]. This suggests the following definition:
be Riemannian manifolds with partial orders
is called a morphism of partially ordered Riemannian manifolds if
is a morphism of Riemannian manifolds and maps any two comparable points of
This defines the category of partially ordered Riemannian manifolds. For example, in finite dimensions consider the set of faithful weights in
. Then an operator monotone bijection on this set transforms
. According to [10], by this means we can get any of the monotone metrics as classified by Petz [13]. Thus all the models are isomorphic when regarded as partially ordered Riemannian manifolds.
In some recent work [1] on quantum Orlicz spaces, use was made of classical Young functions. Thus, if

In [5], the authors associate with a Banach manifold
a whole bundle of tangent spaces, coming from the various Amari embeddings,

We now consider the quantum analogue of the inequality (16): the classical Young function
is continuous and increasing where finite. It follows that the infimum in theorem (7) is achieved at

which multiplies up to give for tangent and cotangent vectors
We have argued that the information manifold in quantum theory should consist of density operators
, some fractional power of which is still of trace class. The topology on the manifold should not be given by the trace norm. Instead, a 'hood of a given state
-bounded quadratic forms; these were shown [8] to make up a possible analogue of the Cramér class of random variables, in that their Kubo-Mori expansion is analytic. This set of states carries the
affine structure of Amari. A possible Young function, related to the free energy, was introduced. The dual Young function was shown to be finite on a set, the union of all
-affine structure of Amari. The beginnings of Young theory (the BKM-metric, the Luxemburg norms, Young's inequality and the Hölder-Orlicz inequality) were derived.
in the dual Luxemburg norm. The quantum Hölder-Orlicz inequality (36) then shows that the bilinear form between the spaces remains finite; we can therefore extend the definition of the

-metric to the completions. The two Banach spaces thus obtained contain only normal states. The tangent and cotangent spaces are then complete and dual relative to the Hilbert-Schmidt scalar product, and are furnished with the
-affine structures. The tangent space then contains the set of operators with finite Araki norm, and the cotangent space contains the states which are perturbations of
The Luxemburg norm becomes large when we add a perturbation such that one of

nearly ceases to be of trace class. In this way, the manifold consists of points that are in the interior of some one-parameter exponential model. All states in the manifold have finite entropy, and states near
One important property of the theory remains unproved: the equivalence of the Luxemburg norms based on points
lying in the overlaps of any

. It would also be nice for the dual affine structures to be defined on the same space. In the classical case, this was resolved by Grasselli [7] in the subtheory obtained by completing the space of bounded perturbations in the Luxemburg norm, to obtain the (separable) Banach space
. Then the information manifold becomes a Banach manifold modelled on

. In the quantum case, the analogue of this space seems to be the completion in the Luxemburg norm of the linear space consisting of perturbations of finite Araki norm. One can ask whether this completion consists of only tiny forms.
